meta.title: 2.7 Framework Support as a Platform Decision
meta.description: Learn what "supporting a framework" requires at the platform level — templates, dependency caches, dev server config, build pipelines, deployment adapters, and LLM training data — and why adding Vue or Svelte multiplies operational complexity faster than demand justifies.

blocks.prompt:
Should Make support React, Vue, Svelte, Angular, AND vanilla JS? Or should it pick one framework and go deep? What are the consequences of each choice? Write your instinct before reading.

---

blocks.content:
## What You'll Learn

By the end of this module, you'll be able to explain why "adding Svelte support" is not a two-day project, articulate the sequencing logic for framework additions using demand and infrastructure readiness signals, and evaluate the breadth-versus-depth tradeoff that separates v0's approach from Bolt's.

Deciding to support a framework is not a product checkbox but an infrastructure commitment across at least seven dimensions — scaffolding, dependency caching, dev server config, build pipeline, deployment adapters, agent training quality, and component library integration — each of which multiplies the platform's operational surface area. It's actually a runtime and infrastructure question with product consequences that compound over time. Understanding what "support a framework" actually requires at the platform level is what separates a PM who can drive these decisions from one who defers them to engineering.

**What "supporting a framework" actually means:**

**1. Scaffolding templates.** When a user starts a new project, Make generates initial files. For React + Vite: `vite.config.ts`, `src/main.tsx`, `src/App.tsx`, `index.html`, `package.json` with the right dependencies. For Next.js: `next.config.js`, `app/page.tsx`, `app/layout.tsx`, `.env.local`, different file conventions. For SvelteKit: `svelte.config.js`, `vite.config.ts`, `src/routes/+page.svelte`. Each framework has its own conventions that the agent must know to initialize correctly.

**2. Dependency pre-caching.** If your preview environment caches common packages to avoid npm install latency, you need separate caches for each framework's dependency set. React's core packages (react, react-dom, @types/react, @vitejs/plugin-react) are different from Vue's (vue, @vue/compiler-sfc, @vitejs/plugin-vue). Cache invalidation strategy multiplies across frameworks.

**3. Dev server configuration.** Vite's config for a React project (`@vitejs/plugin-react`) differs from Vue (`@vitejs/plugin-vue`). Next.js uses its own dev server, not raw Vite. SvelteKit uses Vite with the SvelteKit plugin. Each requires different configuration in the execution environment. If your preview infrastructure provisions environments with pre-configured dev servers, you're provisioning different environment types per framework.

**4. Build pipeline.** Production builds work differently: Vite's `build` command vs. `next build` vs. `svelte-kit build`. Output formats differ: Vite outputs to `dist/`, Next.js to `.next/`, SvelteKit's output depends on the adapter. Your deployment pipeline must handle each variant.

**5. Deployment adapters.** When Make deploys an app, the deployment target integration differs per framework. Next.js deploys cleanly to Vercel (first-party integration) or AWS Lambda with the right handler. SvelteKit needs an adapter (`adapter-vercel`, `adapter-cloudflare`, `adapter-node`) — a plugin that converts the SvelteKit build output into the format expected by a specific hosting platform. Remix uses similar adapter patterns. Each adapter must be tested and maintained.

**6. Agent training and prompting.** The LLM that generates code has been trained on vast amounts of code from the internet. React has a massive training corpus — React patterns are well-represented. Svelte has less training data, which means the agent generates lower-quality, less idiomatic Svelte code. Vue falls in between. Angular has unique patterns (dependency injection — a design pattern where a component declares what it needs and the framework provides it, rather than the component creating its own dependencies; decorators — special syntax that wraps a class or function to add behavior; zone.js — Angular's library for tracking asynchronous operations) that models handle poorly unless explicitly prompted. The quality of generated code is a function of training data volume, and React wins this by orders of magnitude today.

**7. Component library integration.** Figma's unique advantage is design systems. If Make can generate code that uses Figma design tokens (named values for colors, spacing, and typography that feed directly into code), that's a differentiated feature. But component libraries are framework-specific: shadcn/ui is React-only, Vuetify is Vue-only, Skeleton is Svelte-only. Figma's ability to export design tokens to component code depends on which framework those components target.

---

blocks.question: A product manager argues: "We should support all major frameworks from day one. Users shouldn't have to think about framework — Make should just generate the right thing for their app." What's wrong with this argument at the platform level, and what condition would have to be true for it to be correct?
blocks.answer:
The argument ignores that "supporting" a framework is not a binary — it's a quality level. Supporting five frameworks at 60% quality is worse than supporting one at 95% quality. The user who gets generated Vue code with subtle React idioms baked in is worse off than a user who's told "we support React" and gets excellent React code. The argument would be correct if: (a) the agent could generate equally high-quality code for all frameworks (currently false due to training data differences), (b) the preview infrastructure could support all framework build tooling identically (currently false due to configuration complexity), and (c) deployment adapters existed for all frameworks with equal reliability (currently false). Framework breadth is an eventual goal, not a day-one strategy.*

---

blocks.content:
**The breadth vs. depth tradeoff in practice:**

**v0 (Vercel)** chose depth: Next.js only. The upside is that v0's Next.js code generation is excellent — it generates idiomatic App Router patterns, correct Server Component usage, proper TypeScript types. v0 understands Next.js conventions deeply because they're the same company. The downside is market exclusion: teams using Vue, Svelte, or plain Vite can't use v0 without migrating frameworks.

**Bolt (StackBlitz)** chose breadth: WebContainers runs any framework that works in Node.js. Bolt will generate React, Vue, Svelte, Astro, vanilla JS — whatever the user requests. The upside is flexibility. The downside is that Bolt's code generation for non-React frameworks is noticeably lower quality. The agent defaults to React patterns even when prompted for Vue, and Vue-specific optimizations are rarely applied correctly.

**The PM decision framework:**

The right sequencing question is: *what framework maximizes the product's ability to deliver value while supporting the eventual breadth?*

**Start with React + Vite** (not Next.js). Reasons:
- Largest agent training corpus → best code quality
- Largest developer user base → broadest potential audience
- Simpler runtime than Next.js (no SSR — Server-Side Rendering — no server components, no deployment complexity) → easier preview infrastructure
- Strong Figma community precedent (React is the dominant Figma plugin/widget framework)

**Add Next.js** when: users are building apps that need SSR, SEO, or server-side logic, and the preview infrastructure can handle the more complex server runtime. This is a meaningful step up in platform complexity.

**Add Vue and Svelte** based on demand signals: community requests, usage data from waitlist surveys, integration opportunities (Nuxt has strong EU market share; SvelteKit has a growing community in certain technical niches).

**Defer Angular** indefinitely or until there's a specific enterprise buyer requiring it. Angular's complexity (dependency injection, zone.js, opinionated project structure) makes agent-generated Angular code particularly error-prone.

**Figma's unique framework leverage:**

The component library integration angle changes the calculus. If Figma invests in design-token-to-component pipelines, the framework that has the best Figma component ecosystem becomes the obvious default. Today that's React — the Figma community has built extensive React component systems (Figma Variables → CSS custom properties → React style props is a worked pattern). This gives Make a reason to go deep on React beyond just training data: it's where the Figma ecosystem already lives.

**Framework support as competitive positioning:**

v0's Next.js depth is a bet that Next.js becomes the dominant framework for AI-generated production apps. Bolt's framework agnosticism is a bet that flexibility matters more than depth. Make's decision is a signal to the developer community about what kind of platform it is — a Next.js IDE or a framework-agnostic generator.

Neither answer is wrong. But the answer shapes hiring (what framework expertise your DevRel and engineering teams need), product roadmap (which component library integrations you prioritize), and go-to-market (which developer communities you court).

---

## How to Think About This

Think of framework support as a franchise, not a feature flag. Each new framework is a new franchise location: it needs its own supply chain (dependency caches), its own trained staff (agent quality), its own equipment (dev server config), and its own distribution network (deployment adapters). Opening five locations before you've perfected the first one means five mediocre experiences instead of one excellent one. The right question is never 'can we support it?' but 'can we support it well enough that users are better off than if we said no?'

blocks.question: The module recommends starting with React + Vite rather than Next.js. An engineer pushes back: 'Next.js is more popular. We should start where adoption is highest.' How do you respond?
blocks.answer: Popularity is a demand signal, not an infrastructure signal. Next.js's App Router requires a running Node.js process for Server Components — the preview infrastructure is meaningfully more complex than for React + Vite, which can be previewed from a static file server. Starting with React + Vite lets the platform establish a stable, low-complexity preview infrastructure before taking on server-side rendering, Server Actions, and the deployment complexity of Next.js. The sequencing argument is: prove the preview model works at scale with the simpler runtime, then add the more complex one. The agent quality argument also applies: React + Vite generates better code than Next.js App Router because the training corpus for idiomatic App Router patterns is much smaller than for plain React.

blocks.title:
Open v0.dev and Bolt.new side by side. Prompt both: "Build a user profile page with an avatar upload, a form to edit name/bio, and a save button." Compare:

1. Which framework does each default to?
2. How idiomatic is the code? Does it use framework-specific patterns correctly?
3. How does the preview start up? What's the time from prompt to visible preview?
4. What happens if you ask for a Vue version of the same thing?

These observations give you direct product intelligence on competitor depth vs. breadth decisions.

---
blocks.solution: v0 should default to Next.js with shadcn/ui components — the code will be idiomatic App Router style with TypeScript and properly typed props. The preview will take 15–40 seconds (it's a full deployment). When you ask for a Vue version, v0 will likely decline or redirect back to React/Next.js — depth means explicit framework focus. Bolt should generate a React + Vite app by default in a WebContainer — preview appears in under 1 second. When you ask for Vue, Bolt will attempt it, but look carefully at the generated code: you may see React patterns (like using `useState` logic patterns translated awkwardly into Vue 3 Composition API, or incorrect component lifecycle usage). The code may run but may not be idiomatic Vue. This contrast is the breadth/depth tradeoff made visible: v0 generates better, more idiomatic code for its supported framework but refuses others; Bolt generates faster previews for more frameworks but at lower idiomatic quality. Your job as PM is to decide which failure mode is worse for your users.

blocks.prompt:
A senior engineer says: "Adding Svelte support is just adding a template and a Vite plugin — it's a two-day project." What are the seven things they're not accounting for in that estimate?

---

questions:
Think of a platform or tool you've worked on that had to decide between broad surface area and deep quality in one area. How did you decide where to go deep first, and looking back, was that the right call?

---

blocks.content:
Framework support is not a product checkbox but an infrastructure commitment — scaffolding, dependency caching, dev server configuration, build pipelines, deployment adapters, and agent quality all multiply per framework — which means the right strategy is to sequence framework additions by demand signal rather than specifying breadth on day one.

---

blocks.prompt:
You now understand the execution stack from runtime selection through framework support. Level 3 moves up the stack to how Make manages the agent that generates the code — how it maintains context across edits, how it evaluates output quality, and how it recovers from failures. How do the runtime constraints you've learned about change what the agent can and cannot do?

---

---

reviewQuestions.question: The module identifies seven dimensions of work required to support a new framework. Which of the following is NOT one of those seven?
options: Deployment adapters that convert the framework's build output to the format expected by a hosting platform
Open-source license review to ensure the framework is compatible with Make's commercial terms
Agent training quality — the LLM generates lower-quality code for frameworks with less training data in its corpus
reviewQuestions.explanation: The module's seven dimensions are: scaffolding templates, dependency pre-caching, dev server configuration, build pipeline, deployment adapters, agent training quality, and component library integration. License review is not listed as one of the seven. Agent training quality is explicitly included because LLMs have much less Svelte or Angular training data than React, producing lower-quality generated code.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right — license review isn't one of the seven. The agent training quality dimension is often the most surprising one for PMs who think of framework support as a purely engineering decision.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: License review is not one of the module's seven dimensions. The seven are: scaffolding templates, dependency pre-caching, dev server configuration, build pipeline, deployment adapters, agent training quality, and component library integration. Agent training quality is explicitly included — LLMs generate worse Svelte code than React code because their training corpus contains far less Svelte.

reviewQuestions.question: The module recommends starting with React + Vite rather than Next.js. What is the infrastructure reason for this sequencing?
options: React + Vite has more total npm downloads than Next.js, indicating higher user demand
Next.js has stricter licensing requirements that create legal risk for Figma
React + Vite is client-side only, allowing a simpler static-file preview environment; Next.js requires a running Node.js server for Server Components and Server Actions
reviewQuestions.explanation: The module's sequencing argument is infrastructure-based: React + Vite is client-side only, so its preview infrastructure can be a static file server — the simplest possible setup. Next.js App Router requires a running Node.js process for Server Components, Server Actions, and route handling. Starting with React + Vite lets the platform establish a stable, simple preview model before taking on the added complexity of server-side rendering.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly — the preview infrastructure simplification is the core sequencing argument, not demand differences or licensing.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Next.js has no licensing issues with Figma, and download counts are a demand signal, not an infrastructure argument. The module's reasoning is: React + Vite is client-side only (simpler, static-file preview), while Next.js requires a running Node.js server for SSR and Server Components. Start simpler, prove the model, then add server-side complexity.

reviewQuestions.question: v0 (Vercel) supports only Next.js. Bolt supports many frameworks. According to the module, why is v0's depth-first approach better for users than Bolt's breadth-first approach?
options: Supporting five frameworks at 60% quality produces code with framework idiom errors that cause runtime bugs — which is worse than telling users the platform only supports one framework
Next.js is more popular than all other frameworks combined, so v0's focus captures more potential users
Breadth requires more server infrastructure, making Bolt economically unsustainable compared to v0
reviewQuestions.explanation: The module's argument is about failure modes: bad framework support is worse than no support. A user who gets generated Vue code with React patterns embedded hits runtime errors they can't diagnose. That user is worse off than a user who was simply told 'we support React' and received correct code. Silent failures from cross-framework pattern bleeding damage user trust more than explicit scope limitations.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's the key insight — silent failure from low-quality support is worse than explicit non-support, which is why framework depth is the right sequencing strategy.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Market size and infrastructure cost aren't the module's core arguments. The quality argument is: 60% framework quality produces code that looks correct but has subtle idiom errors from pattern bleeding between frameworks. Users hit runtime errors they don't understand. That's a worse outcome than a clear 'we support React only' message.
